book_claims.mechanism: Complete protein with all essential amino acids; rich in B vitamins, choline, and fat-soluble vitamins
book_claims.text: Eggs consumed by centenarians over age 65 (e.g., Emma Morano ate 3 eggs/day) and may support muscle mass preservation in older adults

book_claims.mechanism: Animal protein raises IGF-1; acceptable trade-off in elderly at risk of sarcopenia
book_claims.text: Eggs are generally avoided in the Longevity Diet but may be added in small amounts in people over 65 to prevent weight and muscle loss

book_claims.mechanism: Egg yolk is a concentrated source of fat-soluble vitamins A and D and B12
book_claims.text: Source of vitamin B12 (0.6 mcg per large hard-boiled, 10% DV), folate, iron, vitamin A, and vitamin D (41 IU per large — found in yolk)

supplementary_research.finding: Egg consumption not associated with increased cardiovascular risk in healthy adults; choline content supports muscle protein synthesis in older adults
Leucine-rich complete proteins like eggs stimulate muscle protein synthesis; beneficial for preventing sarcopenia after age 65

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Highest PDCAAS among common foods; all essential amino acids
Primarily in yolk; supports liver health and neurological function
Located in yolk; enhanced by pasture-raising

synergies.note: Both recommended animal proteins for those over 65 or losing muscle mass
Legumes are preferred protein source earlier in life; eggs introduced only in old age as exception
Fat in egg yolk improves absorption of fat-soluble vitamins (A, K, E) from vegetables
